title: Splenic artery compression: short gastric arteries ischemic
vignette: A 56-year-old man comes to the physician complaining of early satiety, nausea, and vague epigastric pain for the past 4 months. He has a 36-pack-year smoking history and admits to drinking alcohol daily for the past 15 years. On physical examination, the patient appears thin and cachectic. Palpation of his abdomen reveals splenomegaly. Abdominal CT scan shows a large irregular mass extending posteriorly from the greater curvature of the stomach, impinging on the splenic artery and vein as they pass below. Tissues supplied by which of the following arteries would most likely be affected by compression of the splenic artery?

clues.text: early satiety, nausea, and vague epigastric pain
clues.note: Early satiety with epigastric symptoms over months suggests a gastric mass (e.g., gastric carcinoma) - the lesion that will compress the splenic vessels.
Heavy smoking is a risk factor for gastric adenocarcinoma, supporting a malignant gastric mass as the compressing lesion.
Cachexia points to an underlying malignancy, consistent with gastric cancer producing the mass.
Splenomegaly here reflects splenic VEIN compression by the mass (impaired splenic venous outflow) - it confirms the lesion involves the splenic vessels.

clues.text: large irregular mass extending posteriorly from the greater curvature of the stomach, impinging on the splenic artery
clues.note: The mass compresses the SPLENIC ARTERY. The branch most vulnerable to the resulting ischemia is the one with the POOREST collateral supply - the short gastric arteries.

whatMatters: The splenic artery gives off the SHORT GASTRIC arteries and the left gastroepiploic artery near the greater curvature. The short gastric arteries have POOR anastomoses, so the gastric fundus they supply is most vulnerable to ischemia if the splenic artery is compressed. The left gastroepiploic is protected by its strong anastomosis with the right gastroepiploic. Answer E.
strategy: When an artery is occluded, the tissue at risk is the one with the WEAKEST collateral (anastomotic) backup. Both short gastric and left gastroepiploic arise from the splenic artery, but only the short gastrics lack good anastomoses - so they're the ones that ischemize. The left gastroepiploic (your pick) is rescued by the right gastroepiploic loop along the greater curvature.

eliminations.label: Gastroduodenal - off the common hepatic, not splenic
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the gastroduodenal artery arises from the COMMON HEPATIC artery and supplies the pylorus and proximal duodenum. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it is not a branch of the splenic artery, so splenic artery compression doesn't threaten its territory. ✅ Correct if: asked about pyloric/duodenal supply or a bleeding posterior duodenal ulcer.

eliminations.label: Left gastroepiploic - splenic branch, but well anastomosed (your pick)
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the left gastroepiploic DOES arise from the splenic artery and runs along the greater curvature. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it has a STRONG anastomosis with the right gastroepiploic artery, so its territory is collaterally supplied and protected from ischemia - unlike the poorly anastomosed short gastrics. The trap is stopping at 'it's a splenic branch' without weighing collaterals. ✅ Correct if: the question asked which branch is most PROTECTED, or about greater-curvature anastomoses.

eliminations.label: Right gastric - off the hepatic/proper hepatic
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the right gastric artery supplies the distal lesser curvature and arises from the proper hepatic (or common hepatic) artery. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: not a splenic artery branch, so it is unaffected by splenic artery compression. ✅ Correct if: asked about lesser-curvature supply via the hepatic system.

eliminations.label: Right gastroepiploic - off the gastroduodenal
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: the right gastroepiploic arises from the gastroduodenal artery and supplies the distal greater curvature. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it derives from the hepatic (celiac) system, not the splenic artery, and it is the collateral that PROTECTS the left gastroepiploic - it isn't the ischemic territory. ✅ Correct if: asked which artery anastomoses with/backs up the left gastroepiploic.

reasoning: A gastric mass compresses the splenic artery (and vein, causing splenomegaly)
The splenic artery's gastric branches are the short gastric and left gastroepiploic arteries
Short gastric arteries have POOR anastomoses; left gastroepiploic has a strong anastomosis with the right gastroepiploic
-> short gastric territory (fundus) is most vulnerable to ischemia; answer E
mechanism: The splenic artery arises from the celiac trunk and runs along the superior pancreas, giving off the SHORT GASTRIC arteries and the left gastroepiploic artery as it nears the greater curvature before reaching the spleen. The short gastric arteries supplying the gastric fundus have very POOR anastomotic connections, so their territory is highly vulnerable to ischemia if splenic arterial inflow is compromised. By contrast, the left gastroepiploic artery is protected because it anastomoses strongly with the right gastroepiploic artery (a gastroduodenal branch) along the greater curvature, providing collateral flow. Therefore, compression of the splenic artery most likely produces ischemia in the short gastric distribution.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Anatomy (GI) > foregut blood supply: splenic artery -> short gastric (poor anastomoses, fundus) + left gastroepiploic (anastomoses with right gastroepiploic); collateral networks determine ischemic vulnerability.

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: The question asks which tissue is most AFFECTED by splenic artery compression - i.e., the splenic branch with the WORST collateral supply (short gastrics), not just any splenic branch.

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on short gastric vs gastroepiploic arteries, anastomosis/collateral circulation, celiac trunk branches, or watershed/poorly anastomosed territory? Tell me which.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Left gastroepiploic (your pick) is a real splenic branch, so it feels right - but its robust anastomosis with the right gastroepiploic protects it; the short gastrics, lacking collaterals, are the ones that ischemize.

difficulty.label: 🖼️ Imaging / diagram
difficulty.message: The upper-abdominal artery diagram maps the celiac branches (short gastric and gastroepiploic arteries off the splenic). If it confused you, the principle is collateral-based: poorest anastomosis (short gastric) = most ischemic.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Bank: splenic artery branches = short gastric (poor anastomoses -> ischemia-prone fundus) + left gastroepiploic (well anastomosed with right gastroepiploic -> protected).

keywords.back: Short gastric arteries + left gastroepiploic artery

keywords.front: Most ischemia-prone branch with splenic artery occlusion
keywords.back: Short gastric arteries (POOR anastomoses; supply fundus)
Strong anastomosis with the right gastroepiploic artery
Gastroduodenal artery (from common hepatic; celiac system)
Celiac trunk; runs superior to the pancreas to the spleen

firstAid.detail: Anatomy > celiac trunk -> splenic artery -> short gastric (poor anastomoses) + left gastroepiploic (anastomoses w/ right gastroepiploic).
firstAid.location: Anatomy > celiac trunk -> splenic artery -> short gastric (poor anastomoses) + left gastroepiploic (anastomoses w/ right gastroepiploic).

firstAid.detail: Anatomy > left/right gastric (lesser curvature), left/right gastroepiploic (greater curvature), short gastrics (fundus).
firstAid.location: Anatomy > left/right gastric (lesser curvature), left/right gastroepiploic (greater curvature), short gastrics (fundus).

firstAid.detail: Anatomy > poorly anastomosed territory (short gastrics) is most vulnerable to ischemia on occlusion.
firstAid.location: Anatomy > poorly anastomosed territory (short gastrics) is most vulnerable to ischemia on occlusion.

memoryHook: Short gastrics = short on backup. They have lousy anastomoses, so when the splenic artery gets squeezed, the fundus they feed is the first to starve.
